rulings.Case Title: MARGARET K REYES VS. RICHARD CURCI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing For Verified First Amended Petition For Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER MARGARET REYES's Notice Of Hearing For Verified First Amended Petition For Writ Of Mandate. Petitioner Margaret Reyes's petition for writ of mandate is denied. The petition is not moot. "A case is moot when the decision of the reviewing court 'can have no practical impact or provide the parties effectual relief.'" (MHC Operating Limited Partnership v. City of San Jose (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 204, 214.) Even if the Court agreed that there is no practical benefit in directing respondent to "correct [petitioner's] record" because of petitioner's subsequent termination, the amended petition also seeks back pay. (Amended Petition, 10:10-19.) Whether petitioner can recover back pay because of the suspensions presents a live controversy for this Court to resolve. The court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for respondents is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE APPLICATION OF: DELOITTE TOUCHE LLP
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Enforce Subpoena For Production Of Documents Pursuant To Ccp 2029.600
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 6, PETITIONER DELOITTE TOUCHE LLP'S Petition To Enforce Subpoena For Production Of Documents Pursuant To CCP 2029.600. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. As Respondent does not agree to stipulate to the Judge Pro Tem, a hearing will be conducted and a report and recommendation will be issued. Parties should be prepared to address the application and implication of C.C.P. Sec. 2025.480(b) and Unzipped Apparel, LLC v. Bader (2007) 156 Cal. App. 4th 123, specifically whether "the completion of record of the deposition" has occurred and if so, when it occurred. Respondent should also be prepared to address the application and implication of C.C.P. Sec. 2020.430(c) and Evid. Code Sec. 1563(b) as raised in the moving and reply papers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, then the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing if the tentative ruling is contested. If the tentative is not contested and there is no hearing on the motion, then Defendant must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: C.I. ENERGIA SOLAR S.A., INC., VS. BAGATELOS GLASS SYSTEMS, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff C.I.Energia Solar S.A.,Inc.'S Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 5, Plaintiff C.I.Energia Solar S.A.,Inc.'s Motion For Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is granted. C.I. Energia Solar S.A. does not and will not need to respond to Swinerton's Special Interrogatories, Set One. Request for sanctions is granted and against Swinerton in the amount of $1,840. The request for sanctions against Swinerton's counsel is denied because the notice of motion did not properly identify the counsel by name as to provide proper notice to counsel. The court finds that Swinerton has failed to meet its burden of justifying the need for 347 special interrogatories as required by C.C.P. Sec. 2030.040(b), especially in light of the fact that it had the opportunity and chose not to participate the depositions of moving party's employees to obtain the information sought by the special interrogatories and that the information sought by the special interrogatories appear to be the substantially the same as the information sought by Requests for Admissions and accompanying Form Interrogatory 17.1. See Weil & Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2019) Sec. 8:1017.1. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, then the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing if the tentative ruling is contested. If the tentative is not contested and there is no hearing on the motion, then Defendant must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: EDUARDO TORRES VS. BAY VIEW PAINTING ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Bay View Painting'S Motion For Reconsideration (Per Ex Parte Order Filed On 7-3-19)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 6, Defendant Bay View Painting's Motion For Reconsideration. Defendant Bay View Painting's motion for reconsideration is granted. The Court exercises its inherent powers to reconsider its prior order. (See Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094.) Upon further reconsideration, the Court grants Defendant's motion to vacate the default entered on August 21, 2018. Code of Civil Procedure § 425.11(c) provides that "the plaintiff shall serve the statement [of damages] on the defendant before a default may be taken." In this case, plaintiff entered default on August 21, 2018 and filed the statement of damages on April 23, 2019. The statement of damages indicates that it was served on July 9, 2018, which was prior to entry of the default. However, the evidence indicates that Plaintiff may have served the statement of damages at the wrong address. Plaintiff served the summons and complaint on Defendant's owner at 466 Milton Avenue, San Bruno and obtained a notice and acknowledgment of receipt. (Massey Affidavit, Ex. B.) In addition, Plaintiff's counsel was told that 466 Milton was Defendant's correct address. (Amended Declaration of David Chen Esq. in Support of Plaintiff Eduardo Torres' Prove-Up, 7.) However, Plaintiff served the statement of damages at 464 Milton Avenue, and contrary to the proof of service in the document itself, there is no signed notice and acknowledgment of receipt. (Massey Affidavit, Ex. D.) Although Plaintiff's counsel declares that his research showed the latter address to be correct, he does not explain why he did not serve the statement of damages at the first, correct address. Given the liberal policy in favor of hearing cases on the merits, the Court concludes that the default should be set aside. (See Fasuyi v. Permatex, Inc. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 681, 692-694 [abuse of discretion for trial court to deny motion to set aside default and default judgment where there was conflicting evidence as to whether statement of damages had been properly served].) Plaintiff's failure to serve the statement of damages prior to entry of the default was clear on the record given the address discrepancy. Accordingly, the default was void and can be set aside at any time. (See Hamm v. Elkin (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 1343 [statement of damages must be served before entry of default]; Plotitsa v. Superior Court (1983) 140 Cal.App.3d 755, 760 ["[A] default entered without any such statement being served on the defendant is void on the face of the record and may be successfully challenged beyond the six-month limitation period specified in section 473."].) Although Defendant did not (and has not) sought to set aside the default on that ground, the Court nevertheless concludes that relief is appropriate. Plaintiff's counsel shall pay Defendant $1,000.00 to reimburse it for the costs it incurred in opposing Plaintiff's ill-founded motion. (Code Civ. Proc. 473(c)(1)(A).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GREGORY BRUNO BOVO VS. ADRIAN ADONA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Mental Examination Of Plaintiff
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT ADRIAN ADONA'S Motion To Compel Mental Examination Of Plaintiff. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is denied. Defendant's Notice of Motion fails to satisfy the requirement of C.C.P. Sec. 2032.310(b) in that it did not state the time, place, identity and specialty of the examiner, and the manner, conditions, scope and nature of the examination. Additionally, the motion was not accompanied by a separate statement of disputed matters as required by Cal. Rules of Court rule 3.1345(a)(6). Moreover, the court finds that the moving party did not make a sufficient reasonable and good faith attempt to arrange the examination by stipulation in that the moving party merely sent emails and did not attempt to meet and confer "in person, by telephone or by letter" C.C.P. Sec. 2023.010(i). The court moreover notes that the trial in this action is schedule for August 26, 2019, and discovery cut-off in this action is July 29, 2019. The court further notes that there is no motion by any party to re-open discovery nor is there a court order re-opening discovery in this action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, then the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing if the tentative ruling is contested. If the tentative is not contested and there is no hearing on the motion, then Defendant must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MUSA JARADIE VS. YIHANG ZHANG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice And Motion To Compel Enforcement Of The Courts June 10,2019 Discovery Order Imposing Terminating Sanction
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT YIHANG ZHANG'S Motion To Compel Enforcement Of The Courts June 10,2019 Discovery Order Imposing Terminating Sanction. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is denied. The court first notes that Defendant/moving party filed two motions, first on July 2 and then on July 5, that appear to be identical with the only notable difference being the time of the hearing on Defendant/moving party's motion(s), and the moving papers filed on July 5 provided the wrong time for the hearing. The proofs of service accompanying moving papers are defective, and Defendant/moving party failed to comply with C.C.P. Secs. 1005 and 1013. The court also note that Defendant failed to properly serve the notice of entry of the Court's June 10, 2019 order upon defendant. It appears Defendant/moving party has been serving papers to Plaintiff but addressing the papers to someone other than Plaintiff. As such, court has serious concerns about Plaintiff's due process rights not being protected/preserved under these circumstances. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, then the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing if the tentative ruling is contested. If the tentative is not contested and there is no hearing on the motion, then Defendant must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL REITERMAN VS. DOES 1 TO 10, INCLUSIVE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Due To Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT FARAH ABID's Motion To Dismiss Due To Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction. Defendant Farah Ali Abid's motion to dismiss is granted. The case is dismissed for lack of personal jurisdiction. To determine whether a defendant is subject to specific personal jurisdiction, the Court considers the affiliation between the forum and the underlying controversy, or whether the defendant has "minimum contacts" with the forum. (Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. v. Superior Court (2017) 137 S.Ct. 1773, 1780-1781.) The Court also evaluates the burden on the defendant in subjecting them to jurisdiction as well as the interests of the forum state, the Plaintiff, and the broader judicial system. (Id.) In opposing Defendant's motion, the burden of proof is on Plaintiff to demonstrate that "minimum contacts" exist between Defendant and the forum state to justify imposition of personal jurisdiction. (Mihlon v. Superior Court (1985) 169 Cal.App.3d 703, 710.) The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: J GARCIA CARRION, SA ET AL VS. VINCENT FRIEND ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, July 29, 2019, Line 12, CROSS DEFENDANT J GARCIA CARRION, SA, CIV USA, INC's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. Cross-Defendants J Garcia Carrion, SA (JGC), CIV USA, Inc., and Jose Garcia Carrion's special motion to strike Cross-Complainant Vincent Friend's first amended cross-complaint pursuant to CCP § 425.16 (anti-SLAPP) is granted. Cross-Defendants successfully show that the first amended cross-complaint (FACC) relies upon protected activity that subjects it to the anti-SLAPP statute. Cross-Complainant fails to establish a probability of success on the merits. The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
